name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَإِن تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا أَءِنَّا لَفِى خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَغْلَـٰلُ فِىٓ أَعْنَاقِهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (٥)
```

```english
If you are to wonder [at anything], then wonderful is their remark, ‘When we have become dust, shall we be [ushered] into a new creation?’ They are the ones who defy their Lord; they shall have iron collars around their necks, they shall be the inhabitants of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]. (5)
```

```arabic
وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ وَقَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِمُ ٱلْمَثُلَـٰتُ ۗ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِّلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ ظُلْمِهِمْ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٦)
```

```english
They would press you for evil sooner than for good, though there have already gone by before them exemplary punishments. Indeed your Lord is forgiving to mankind despite their wrongdoing, and indeed your Lord is severe in retribution. (6)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a conjunction (عطف) to some of what the polytheists (المشركون) used to utter in rejection of the Call (الدعوة) and the Message (الرسالة), such as their saying: "How is it possible to resurrect man after his death and his becoming dust?" and their saying: "Why has the punishment with which he warns us not been sent down upon us?" and "When is this promise [to be fulfilled], if you are truthful?" The response to this is provided in a manner suited to the context.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ أَإِذا كُنَّا تُراباً أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** "If you wonder, then wondrous is their saying, ‘When we are dust, shall we be [raised] in a new creation?’" (13:5) until the end of the verse. It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Wonder (العجب) and wondering (التعجب) is the sudden arrival upon the soul of that whose cause is unknown; and the shackle (الغل) is a collar with which the hand is bound to the neck." End quote.

At the opening of His discourse, the Exalted pointed to the truth of the religious knowledge (معارف الدين) He revealed to His Prophet in His Book, indicating that the signs of creation (آيات التكوين) lead to it and point toward it—its foundations being monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* —, the Message (الرسالة), and the Resurrection (البعث). He then detailed the discourse on the indication of the creative signs toward that, and concluded from three arguments He mentioned the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and the Resurrection (البعث) explicitly. This necessitates the truth of the Message (الرسالة) and the revealed Book, which is its sign (آية).

When this became clear and illuminated, the path was paved to mention the doubts of the disbelievers (الكفار) regarding what pertains to the three foundations. In this verse, He pointed to their doubt regarding the Resurrection (البعث), and He will address their doubts and sayings regarding the Message (الرسالة) and monotheism (التوحيد) in the subsequent verses.

Their doubt in this regard was their saying: **أَإِذا كُنَّا تُراباً أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** "When we are dust, shall we be [raised] in a new creation?" He presented it as something wondrous (عجب), more deserving of wonder due to its manifest falsehood and corruption—a manifestness that leaves no justification for a person of sound intellect to doubt it. Thus, if a human were to utter it, it would be a cause for wonder. So He said: **وَإِنْ تَعْجَبْ فَعَجَبٌ قَوْلُهُمْ** "If you wonder, then wondrous is their saying..."

The meaning of the sentence, as guided by the omission of the object of "wonder" (تَعْجَبْ), is: "If wonder occurs from you—and it must occur because man is never devoid of it—then this saying of theirs is wondrous and your wonder must attach to it." Thus, the construction is a metonymy (كناية) for the necessity of wondering at this saying of theirs, because it is a saying of manifest falsehood that no person of intellect and reason (حجى) would incline toward.

As for their saying: **أَإِذا كُنَّا تُراباً أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيد** "When we are dust, shall we be [raised] in a new creation?", they mean by "dust" (تراب), by the context of the passage, that which the human body turns into after death in the form of dust, at which point the human—who is that specific fleshy frame (هيكل) composed of specific organs and equipped with material faculties, according to their claim—ceases to exist. How then can creation encompass a thing that has vanished from its very root, such that it returns as a new creation?

There are various aspects to this doubt of theirs, and Allah, the Glorified, has responded in His discourse to each one of them in a manner that suits it and severs its root:

Among them is the deeming it improbable (استبعاد) that dust could transform into a sound human being. This has been answered by the fact that the possibility of the transformation of earthly materials into semen (مني), then the semen into a clinging mass (علقة), then the clinging mass into a lump of flesh (مضغة), then the lump of flesh into the body of a sound human being—and the occurrence of this after its possibility—leaves no doubt regarding the permissibility of dust becoming a sound human being a second time. The Exalted said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ فِي رَيْبٍ مِنَ الْبَعْثِ فَإِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ عَلَقَةٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ مُضْغَةٍ مُخَلَّقَةٍ وَغَيْرِ مُخَلَّقَةٍ** "O mankind! If you are in doubt about the Resurrection, [consider that] We created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then from a clinging mass, then from a fleshy tissue, partly formed and partly unformed..." (22:5).

Among them is the deeming it improbable to bring a thing into existence after its non-existence (عدم). This was answered by the fact that it is like the first creation, so it should be permissible just as that was permissible. The Exalted said: **وَضَرَبَ لَنا مَثَلاً وَنَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ** "He draws an example for Us, and forgets his own creation. He says, ‘Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?’ Say, ‘He will revive them who produced them the first time...’" (36:78-79).

Among them is the claim that the human essence (ذات) is negated by death, so there is no essence to be clothed in a new creation, and there is no human after death and passing except in the conception of the conceiver, not in external reality (الخارج) in any way.

This has been answered in the discourse of the Exalted by explaining that the human is not the body composed of several material organs such that he would vanish from his root by the corruption and dissolution of the composition. Rather, his reality is a celestial spirit (روح علوية)—or if you wish, say: a soul (نفس)—attached to this material compound, using it for its purposes and goals. Through it is the life of the body, and through it the human remains with his identity (الشخصية) preserved, even if his body changes and is replaced with the passage of years and the elapsing of life. Then, death is that Allah takes it [the soul] from the body and its attachment to it is severed. Then, the Resurrection (البعث) is that Allah renews the creation of the body and its attachment to it, which is the standing before Allah for the final judgment (فصل القضاء).

The Exalted said: **وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we be [raised] in a new creation?’ Rather they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you fully, then you will be brought back to your Lord.’" (32:10-11). He says that you do not get lost in the earth nor do you vanish through death; rather, the angel entrusted with death takes the thing indicated by the word "you" (كم and نا), which are the souls (النفوس). Thus, they remain in his grasp and are not lost. Then, when you are resurrected, you return to Allah by the joining of your bodies to your souls, and you are [still] you.

Thus, for man there is a remaining life not limited by what is in this vanishing world, and he has a living in another abode that remains by the remaining of Allah. He shall not enjoy his second life except through what he acquired in his first life of faith (الإيمان) in Allah and righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة), and what he prepares in his today for his tomorrow of the materials of felicity (السعادة). If he follows the truth and believes in the signs of Allah, he will be felicitous in his hereafter with the honor of proximity and nearness, and a kingdom that does not perish. But if he clings to the earth, devotes himself to the world, and turns away from the Remembrance (الذكرى), he remains in the abode of misery and ruin, shackled with the shackles of disappointment and loss in the place of descent of the curse and the abyss of distance, and he will be among the inmates of the Fire.

Once you have recognized what we have prefaced and contemplated it sufficiently, it will become clear to you that the saying of the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ** "They are the ones who disbelieve in their Lord..." (13:5) until the end of the verse, is not merely a threat of punishment for those sayers: **أَإِذا كُنَّا تُراباً أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** "When we are dust, shall we be [raised] in a new creation?", as might be imagined at first glance. Rather, it is a response based on the necessary implication (لازم) of the statement.

To clarify this: the necessary implication of their saying that when man dies and becomes dust, humanity is nullified and identity (الشخصية) vanishes, is that man is a material form subsisting through this material bodily frame living a material life, without having another eternal life after death in which he remains by the remaining of the Lord, the Exalted, and finds felicity in His proximity and success with Him. In other words, his life is limited to this material life without extending to what is after death and lasting forever. This, in reality, is a denial of the Divine Realm (العالم الربوبي), for there is no meaning to a Lord to Whom there is no return (معاد).

The necessary implication of this is that man restricts his concern to worldly objectives and material goals, without his understanding rising to the permanent bliss and the great kingdom that is with Allah. Thus, he does not strive for proximity to the Exalted nor work in his today for his tomorrow, like one shackled (المغلول) who is unable to move or strive for his necessary affairs.

The necessary implication of this is that man is fixed in a necessary misery and permanent punishment, for he has corrupted the potential for felicity and cut off the path. These three implications are what the Exalted pointed to by His saying: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** "They are the ones who disbelieve..." etc.

So His saying: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ** "They are the ones who disbelieve in their Lord" is an indication of the first implication, which is the turning away of those who deny the Return (المعاد) from the Divine Realm and the remaining life, and the covering up (الستر) of the permanent bliss that is with Allah, and disbelieving in Him.

His saying: **وَأُولئِكَ الْأَغْلالُ فِي أَعْناقِهِمْ** "and they are the ones who will have iron collars around their necks" (13:5) is an indication of the second implication, which is the clinging to the earth, the inclining toward passion (الهوى), and being bound by the bonds of ignorance and the shackles of rejection and denial. There has already passed, in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَسْتَحْيِي أَنْ يَضْرِبَ مَثَلاً** "Indeed Allah is not ashamed to draw a parable..." (2:26) in the first volume of the book, a discussion on whether these Quranic expressions are realities (حقائق) or metaphors (مجازات); so refer to it.

His saying: **أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ** "they shall be the inmates of the Fire, and they shall remain in it [forever]" (13:5) is an indication of the third implication, which is their remaining in punishment and misery.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ الْحَسَنَةِ وَقَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمُ الْمَثُلاتُ** "They bid you to hasten the ill before the good, though there have already been exemplary punishments before them..." (13:6) until the end of the verse. It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Seeking haste (الاستعجال) is to ask for the hastening of a matter, and hastening (التعجيل) is bringing a matter forward before its time. The ill deed (السيئة) is a trait that grieves the soul, and its opposite is the good deed (الحسنة), which is a trait that pleases the soul. Exemplary punishments (المثلات) are punishments; the singular is *mathulah* (مثلة) with a *fathah* on the *mim* and a *dammah* on the *tha*. Whoever says for the singular *muthlah* (مثلة) with a *dammah* on the *mim* and a *sukun* on the *tha*, says for the plural *muthulat* (مثلات) with two *dammahs*, like *ghurfah* and *ghurufat*. It is also said for the plural *mathulat* and *mathalat*—meaning with a *sukun* or *fathah* on the *tha*." End quote.

Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "*Al-Mathulah* is a vengeance that descends upon a person, making him an example (مثال) by which others are deterred, and that is like an exemplary punishment (النكال). Its plural is *mathulat* and *muthulat*—meaning with a *dammah* or *fathah* on the *mim* and a *dammah* on the *tha*. It has also been recited: *min qablihimu al-mathulat*, and *al-mathlat* with the *sukun* of the *tha* for lightening, like *‘adud* and *‘ad-d*." End quote.

His saying: **يَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ الْحَسَنَةِ** "They bid you to hasten the ill before the good"—the plural pronoun refers to those who disbelieved mentioned in the previous verse. What is meant by their hastening the ill before the good is their asking for the descent of punishment upon them in mockery of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) before asking for mercy and well-being. The evidence for this is His saying: **وَقَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمُ الْمَثُلاتُ** "though there have already been exemplary punishments before them"—and the sentence is in the position of a circumstantial clause (حال)—for what is meant by it are the punishments that descended upon past nations, cutting off their roots.

The meaning is: those who disbelieve ask you to bring down upon them the divine punishment before mercy and well-being, after they heard you warning them of Allah's punishment, out of mockery, while they are aware of the punishments that descended before them upon past nations who disbelieved in their messengers. The verse is in the position of expressing wonder (التعجيب).

His saying: **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِلنَّاسِ عَلى ظُلْمِهِمْ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ** "Indeed your Lord is forgiving to mankind despite their wrongdoing, and indeed your Lord is severe in retribution" (13:6) is an initial sentence (استئناف) or in the position of a circumstantial clause. It provides an explanation of the reason why their hastening is a wondrous matter; that is: indeed your Lord is the Possessor of a vast mercy that encompasses people in all their states, even in the state of their wrongdoing (ظلم), and the Possessor of severe wrath, and His mercy has preceded His wrath. So what is the matter with them that they turn away from His vast mercy and forgiveness (المغفرة) and ask for His severe punishment while they are in haste? Indeed, that is wondrous.

From this meaning provided by the context, several points appear:

First: That the expression referring to Him, the Exalted, as **رَبَّكَ** "your Lord" is only to indicate that they are polytheistic idolaters who do not take Him, the Exalted, as a Lord; rather, it is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who takes Him as a Lord from among his people.

Second: That what is meant by forgiveness (المغفرة) and punishment (العقاب) is more general than worldly forgiveness and punishment, for the polytheists were only hastening the worldly ill and punishment, and the exemplary punishments (المثلات) that Allah mentions have passed before them are only the worldly punishments that descended upon them.

Furthermore, pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة) are not restricted to what is after death or the Day of Resurrection, nor are their effects restricted to that. This has been mentioned repeatedly. Thus, He, the Exalted, may extend His forgiveness over whomever He wills, even over the wrongdoer (الظالم) while he is a wrongdoer, forgiving him his wrongdoing if wisdom necessitates it; and He may punish. The Exalted said: **إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبادُكَ وَإِنْ تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** "If You punish them, they are indeed Your creatures; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise." (5:118).

For this subtle point, the Exalted expressed the object of forgiveness by saying: **لِلنَّاسِ** "to mankind" and did not say "to the believers" or "to the repentant" and the like. Thus, if any one of the people were to take refuge in His mercy and ask Him for forgiveness, He would be able to forgive him, whether he be a disbeliever or a believer, and whether for major or minor sins. However, if the polytheist (المشرك) were to ask Him to forgive him his polytheism (الشرك), he would thereby turn into a believer, not a polytheist. And Allah, the Glorified, does not forgive the polytheist as long as he does not return to monotheism (التوحيد). The Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ** "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him, but He forgives anything besides that to whomever He wishes." (4:48).

So it was upon these who disbelieved to ask Him, the Exalted—and to hasten toward Him—to forgive them their polytheism or the sins that branch out from their polytheism by putting forward faith in Him and His Messenger, or to ask Him for well-being, blessing, and good in wealth and children despite their being wrongdoers. For He, by His vast mercy, does that even for one who does not believe in Him nor submit to Him. As for wrongdoing (الظلم) while the wrongdoer is clothed in it, forgiveness does not coexist with it, and the Exalted has said: **وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** "and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot." (62:5).

Third: That His saying: **لَذُو مغفرة** "is the Possessor of forgiveness" and He did not say "is Forgiving" (لغفور) or "Forgiver" (غافرة), is as if to guard against indicating the actuality (فعلية) of forgiveness for all wrongdoers despite their wrongdoing. It is as if it were said: He has forgiveness for people despite their wrongdoing, and nothing prevents Him from exercising this forgiveness when there is a benefit (مصلحة).

Another meaning can be derived from the sentence, which is that He, the Exalted, possesses the forgiveness of people—He may forgive with it whomever He wills of them—and the wrongdoing of people does not necessitate that the Exalted should become angry such that He leaves the attribution of forgiveness from its root and forgives no one. This would necessitate a change in some of what preceded of the subtle points of the verse, but it is not apparent from the context.

In the verse, there are disputes between the Mu‘tazilites and others from the People of the Sunnah (أهل السنة), yet it is absolute (مطلقة), and there is no evidence to restrict it by anything except what is in the saying of the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ** "Indeed Allah does not forgive that any partner be ascribed to Him..." (4:48).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding: **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ لِلنَّاسِ عَلى ظُلْمِهِمْ ـ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَشَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ** "Indeed your Lord is forgiving to mankind despite their wrongdoing, and indeed your Lord is severe in retribution." He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: **لو لا عفو الله وتجاوزه ما هنأ لأحد العيش ، ولو لا وعيده وعقابه لا تكل كل أحد** — "Were it not for Allah's pardon and His overlooking, life would not be pleasant for anyone; and were it not for His threat and His punishment, everyone would have relied [on His mercy without action]."
